topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a vibrant, joyful wave washing over the ballroom. I stood near the edge of the crowd, nursing a lukewarm glass of champagne, my gaze snagging on a familiar silhouette across the room. It was him. My ex. He was laughing, a bright, open sound that seemed to vibrate with an ease I suddenly couldn't access. Every time his arm brushed against his new spouse's, a tight knot formed beneath my ribs, constricting my breath. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that felt like cracking glass on my face, and deliberately turned my attention to a potted palm, focusing intently on the glossy green leaves, anything to avoid meeting his eyes again. The sheer perfection of the scene—the elegant attire, the effortless connection—felt like a physical blow. I took a sharp, shallow breath, the air suddenly too thin, and retreated toward the quieter periphery of the room, needing space from that undeniable warmth radiating from them.

[story 2]

"Oh, look who it is!" Sarah chirped, nudging me forward with an elbow. I managed a weak chuckle, my eyes darting toward the entrance. And there he was, standing near the buffet table, looking impossibly happy. He caught my eye for a fleeting second, and the brief flicker of recognition sent a sharp, unpleasant jolt through me. I watched as he engaged in easy conversation with someone—a shared glance, a relaxed lean—and a distinct tension coiled in my jaw. I felt a sudden, intense urge to move, to disrupt the equilibrium, to inject some discord into their perfect tableau. Instead, I just stood rooted, my fingers tightening around my clutch bag until my knuckles were white, cataloging every detail of his interaction, searching for some flaw, some crack in the facade that would explain why this felt so profoundly wrong.

[story 3]

I pretended to be engrossed in a conversation with a distant acquaintance, but my peripheral vision was entirely dedicated to the couple. They moved as one unit, radiating a kind of effortless belonging that felt utterly inaccessible to me. When he turned slightly, and his gaze swept across the room, it landed on me for a fraction of a second. That flicker—it was too much. It was a reminder of a chapter closed, yet somehow, the sight of him thriving, completely integrated into this new reality, left a hollow ache in my chest. I felt a distinct heat rise in my cheeks, a flush of irritation that had nothing to do with the celebratory atmosphere. I consciously adjusted my posture, pulling my shoulders back, trying to project an air of nonchalance, but inside, a simmering resentment began to bubble up, a hot, acidic feeling that made me want to physically turn away from the entire spectacle.
